<findings>

[observation] [warning] 12 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
[observation] [info] 12 distinct entry points suggest this zone exposes too broad a surface — consolidating through a barrel index would reduce the coupling surface to web-viewer consumers.
[observation] [warning] Bidirectional imports with web-viewer (web imports web-viewer × 23, web-viewer imports web × 7) create a cycle risk — verify these do not form a runtime circular dependency in the bundled output.
[observation] [info] Build config files (build.js, dev.js) are co-located with UI components in this zone; separating build infrastructure from runtime components would improve cohesion.
[suggestion] [info] Zone "web-dashboard-platform" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
[suggestion] [warning] Audit the 12 entry points to determine which are prd-tree exports (which would move with reclassification) vs. genuine platform-level exports. If fewer than 4 entry points remain after prd-tree moves, the residual zone is a dissolution candidate rather than a consolidation target.
[suggestion] [info] The 36% entry-point ratio (12 of 33 files are entry points) should be flagged as a zone surface-area smell. Zones with entry-point ratios above 25% typically lack focused responsibility — add an entry-point count assertion to governance documentation to prevent new entry points being added without review.
[pattern] [warning] Build infrastructure files (build.js, dev.js) are grouped into this zone alongside runtime components purely by directory proximity — their presence corrupts cohesion and coupling metrics for this zone and should be excluded from zone analysis via sourcevision config
[relationship] [critical] web-dashboard-platform → web-viewer (7 imports) combined with web-viewer → web-dashboard-platform (23 imports) forms a runtime circular dependency in the Preact bundle; Preact/Vite cannot resolve initialization order across circular imports, making this a potential runtime crash vector
[anti-pattern] [warning] packages/web/src/viewer/components/prd-tree/ is classified into web-dashboard-platform by zone detection but has 11 production consumers and 26 test consumers all inside src/viewer/. A directory whose entire consumer graph lives in a sibling zone is misclassified — prd-tree should be a sub-directory of the web-viewer zone. Reclassifying it would eliminate the 7 outbound imports that create the bidirectional cycle between web-dashboard-platform and web-viewer.

</findings>

<insights>

- 12 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
- The moderate cohesion (0.53) reflects heterogeneous content — build config files (build.js, dev.js) are algorithmically grouped with UI components, inflating apparent coupling.
- A bidirectional import relationship with web-viewer (23 imports in, 7 imports out) creates a soft cycle that should be audited to ensure no runtime circular dependency exists.
- Entry points include 12 exported surfaces spanning tree search, views, polling, hooks, and usage constants — this zone acts as a secondary hub rather than a focused feature boundary.
- Bidirectional imports with web-viewer (web imports web-viewer × 23, web-viewer imports web × 7) create a cycle risk — verify these do not form a runtime circular dependency in the bundled output.
- Build config files (build.js, dev.js) are co-located with UI components in this zone; separating build infrastructure from runtime components would improve cohesion.
- 12 distinct entry points suggest this zone exposes too broad a surface — consolidating through a barrel index would reduce the coupling surface to web-viewer consumers.
- Zone "web-dashboard-platform" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
- The 7 outbound imports from web-dashboard-platform into web-viewer combined with 23 inbound create a true runtime cycle risk in the bundled Preact output — tree-shaking cannot break cycles, so this may cause initialization-order failures at runtime
- Build config files (build.js, dev.js) share zone membership with runtime UI components solely because they share a directory — this inflates the zone's apparent coupling and makes its cohesion score unreliable as a structural signal
- web-dashboard-platform → web-viewer (7 imports) combined with web-viewer → web-dashboard-platform (23 imports) forms a runtime circular dependency in the Preact bundle; Preact/Vite cannot resolve initialization order across circular imports, making this a potential runtime crash vector
- Build infrastructure files (build.js, dev.js) are grouped into this zone alongside runtime components purely by directory proximity — their presence corrupts cohesion and coupling metrics for this zone and should be excluded from zone analysis via sourcevision config
- All 37 consumers of prd-tree (11 production: views, hooks, components + 26 tests) are exclusively within src/viewer/ — the prd-tree directory is a viewer sub-system with no cross-zone consumers. The community detection algorithm classified prd-tree into web-dashboard-platform solely due to directory proximity or shared dependency chains, but the consumer graph places it firmly in web-viewer. This is a zone classification mismatch, not a coupling problem.
- The 7 outbound imports from web-dashboard-platform to web-viewer are sourced entirely from prd-tree consumers — if prd-tree were reclassified into web-viewer (matching its actual consumer graph), the 7 outbound imports would become intra-zone, eliminating the bidirectional cycle with web-viewer entirely and reducing the cross-zone edge count by 7.
- packages/web/src/viewer/components/prd-tree/ is classified into web-dashboard-platform by zone detection but has 11 production consumers and 26 test consumers all inside src/viewer/. A directory whose entire consumer graph lives in a sibling zone is misclassified — prd-tree should be a sub-directory of the web-viewer zone. Reclassifying it would eliminate the 7 outbound imports that create the bidirectional cycle between web-dashboard-platform and web-viewer.
- 12 entry points out of 33 total files is a 36% entry-point ratio — the highest of any production zone in the codebase. A ratio above 30% indicates the zone exports more surface than it internalizes, functioning as a distribution mechanism rather than a self-contained feature boundary. This structural property is independent of the prd-tree misclassification and would persist even after reclassification.
- If prd-tree (11 production consumers, all in web-viewer) were reclassified into web-viewer, the remaining web-dashboard-platform zone would contain primarily build infrastructure (build.js, dev.js) and a handful of shared platform utilities — a zone too small and heterogeneous to sustain meaningful cohesion metrics. Post-reclassification, the residual zone should be dissolved rather than retained as a stub.
- Audit the 12 entry points to determine which are prd-tree exports (which would move with reclassification) vs. genuine platform-level exports. If fewer than 4 entry points remain after prd-tree moves, the residual zone is a dissolution candidate rather than a consolidation target.
- The 36% entry-point ratio (12 of 33 files are entry points) should be flagged as a zone surface-area smell. Zones with entry-point ratios above 25% typically lack focused responsibility — add an entry-point count assertion to governance documentation to prevent new entry points being added without review.
- [call graph] 775 internal calls, 80 outgoing, 7 incoming (cohesion: 0.91, coupling: 0.09)

</insights>
